objections.objection: The implementation timeline and steps assume the system can rapidly supply and retrain teachers for multigrade pedagogy within a 1–4 year window, but the dossier itself documents an aging workforce (≈50% of primary teachers 50+, only 7.2% under 30) and higher unqualified-teacher rates in disadvantaged rural schools. This makes the proposed Ministry launch + 4-year pipeline insufficient: recruitment/qualification effects will lag, retention will fail without immediate incentives, and tankerületi administrative capacity to authorize/fund multigrade formation is not resourced in the steps. The plan omits concrete short-term supply measures (e.g., temporary certified aides, expedited in-service upskilling, emergency hiring allowances) and measurable teacher-placement targets, so the multigrade classroom rollout risks collapsing into under-qualified, overstretched staffing. (speculative)
objections.suggested_revision: Revise implementation_steps to (a) add immediate interim staffing measures: 12–24 month certified teaching-aide contracts and funded traveling-teacher stipends; (b) specify quantified recruitment/retention targets (e.g., number of new hires per region, vacancy-rate reductions) and KPIs tied to tranche funding; (c) budget and publish a multi-year teacher-incentives envelope scaled to vacancy data (year 0–2 bridge funding plus year 3–6 stabilization); (d) convert the single ‘launch training modules’ line into a phased provider procurement and credential-shortening program (fast-track in-service certification with defined competencies) with explicit timelines and fidelity-monitoring responsibilities for tankerületi központs.

objections.objection: The transport-guarantee and receiving-site readiness steps underestimate legal and municipal-funding lead times. The scenario notes transport is currently an unsubsidized municipal task and cites a Délegyháza-like route costing ~35M HUF/year, yet the steps rely on tankerületi/Ministry negotiation and a year 2–4 timeline to legislate and fund guarantees. In practice, (a) statutory subsidy design, budget appropriation, procurement of vehicles/contracts, and municipal route contracting each take multiple procurement cycles and budget years; (b) safety certifications and union-negotiated duty-time changes for long-route drivers add further delay. The step 'legislate and fund statutory transport guarantees' within two years therefore underestimates legal lead time and the need for bridge funding to operate routes during procurement. (speculative)
objections.suggested_revision: Change implementation_steps to require a two-phase transport plan: Phase A (immediate, months 0–12) — emergency bridging fund to subsidize existing municipal routes and pilot contracted routes for affected villages; Phase B (design + legislation, months 6–30) — national subsidy formula drafted with MOF, procurement framework for route operators, vehicle-safety certification timeline, and union consultation; include a detailed budget estimate and earmarked multi-year appropriation before any upper-grade transfers. Extend timeline for legally binding guarantees to 3–5 years and require published procurement and route-operation milestones before transfers occur.

objections.objection: The step to 'Draft and pass legal amendments creating a Hungarian association-run/network-school category' in year 1–3 neglects two concrete capacity constraints documented in the dossier: (1) post-2013 centralization removed municipal maintainer authority and administrative know-how, so municipalities/associations lack immediate operational capacity to become maintainers; (2) recent legal moves (e.g., 2023 building-transfer law) create asset-security risks for any non-state maintainer. The proposal omits a required legal-feasibility assessment, an explicit compensation/asset-use protocol, and funded administrative-capacity-building for candidate associations—making a 1–3 year legislative-plus-rollout unrealistic and politically fragile.
objections.suggested_revision: Revise implementation_steps to (a) require a formal legal-feasibility and asset-risk assessment (Ministry of Education + Justice + Ombudsman) before drafting amendments; (b) add a funded municipal/association capacity-building tranche (back-office grants, payroll-administration support, compliance helpdesks) to run for at least 3 years alongside pilots; (c) schedule a phased pilot (2–4 regions) with ministry secondments embedded in local administrations to transfer know-how; (d) include explicit safeguards on building-use rights (binding covenants or lease-back clauses) to prevent reversal under existing asset-transfer laws; and expand the legislative timeline to 3–6 years with go/no-go decision gates tied to pilot outcomes.

objections.objection: The proposal to 'Legislate a binding statutory minimum-viability threshold' in year 1–2 and build a disaggregated closure/outcome registry by year 2–5 underestimates legal-data protections and cross-ministry coordination needs. The dossier flags GDPR-like compliance and political sensitivity (Roma/non-Roma disaggregation) yet the steps do not mandate a formal data-protection impact assessment, allocation of a permanent budget line, or a legal appeals mechanism tied to any threshold — leaving the registry vulnerable to legal challenge or selective use by tankerületi döntések. The timeline is optimistic given comparable Hungarian transparency projects have taken multiple years to clear legal and technical hurdles.
objections.suggested_revision: Strengthen implementation_steps by (a) inserting a mandatory Data-Protection Impact Assessment and legal-review step (Ministry of Justice + Data-Protection Authority) prior to registry build; (b) require parliamentary approval of a dedicated multi-year budget line for registry operation before any legislative threshold is enacted; (c) couple any binding threshold to a statutory, enforceable parental appeal procedure and clear judicial/administrative review standard to prevent selective invocation; (d) rephase timeline: years 1–2 legal/design and budget appropriation, years 2–6 technical build and staged public release, with an independent audit at year 4 before nationwide reliance on thresholds.
